_doc.purpose: The RIVALS table. aliases.json solves one axis of collision (one algorithm, many names); this file solves the other (one problem, many phrasings). Each entry's `d` is a specific problem phrase chosen to carry the trade-off ('Negative-edge shortest paths' is exactly why you reach for Bellman-Ford). That specificity is worth keeping, but it fragments the problem axis: Dijkstra and Bellman-Ford stopped looking like rivals because their phrases differ. This registry maps many phrases onto one canonical problem, so rival lookup is mechanical without flattening the phrasing.
_doc.schema: Every key except those starting with underscore is a slug (lowercase, hyphens) that will become a stable /problem/<slug> URL. Value: { label: display name, phrases: [exact `d` values from atlas entries] }. A phrase must match at least one live entry (check fails on dead phrases) and may be claimed by exactly one problem.
_doc.rivalsRule: Two entries are RIVALS when they resolve to the same problem, either through this registry or by sharing an identical `d`. Unit pages name two or three rivals with a when-to-prefer line; see the rivals doctrine in CLAUDE.md and docs/ATLAS.md.
_doc.growthNote: Partial coverage is fine and expected. An unregistered phrase simply stands alone (or clusters with exact matches of itself). `npm run check` prints the largest unregistered phrases as a planning queue, so this file grows from evidence rather than guesswork.
